title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 2 discussion

answers.A: Delete the state file every time you run Terraform.
answers.B: Store the state in an encrypted backend.
answers.C: Always store your secrets in a secrets.tfvars file.
answers.D: Edit your state file to scrub out the sensitive data.

question_text: Your security team scanned some Terraform workspaces and found secrets stored in plaintext in state files. How can you protect that data?

discussion.author: hbouachir
discussion.content: B: Store the state in an encrypted backend because Terraform’s state file contains all the information about your infrastructure, including sensitive values like passwords, API keys, and database credentials. By default, if the state file is stored locally, it’s just a plaintext file—anyone with access can read those secrets.

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 3 discussion
question_text: You can reference a resource created with for_each using a splat ( * ) expression.

discussion.author: purink_101
discussion.content: According to official Terraform documentation, you cannot reference a resource created with for_each using a splat ([*]) expression directly.

discussion.author: brugmany
discussion.content: It was a brain-buster, but I finally figured it out: you can’t use the splat operator to access the entire resource object.

discussion.author: Slimane_lam
Babustest
discussion.content: Splat doesn't work for 'for_each*

discussion.author: Ranjanaj
Cccb35
discussion.content: Count has as result a list and for_each has as result a map. When is a list, you can use (*), however when is a map is not posible use  (*)

discussion.author: sadasdsadawer123235453
sabrthor
discussion.content:
Resources that use the for_each argument will appear in expressions as a map of objects, so you can't use splat expressions with those resources.
To get a similar result with a map or object value you must use for expressions.

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 4 discussion

answers.A: Terraform will use the latest version of the provider for the new resource and the version recorded in the lock file to manage existing resources.
answers.B: Terraform will use the version recorded in your lock file.
answers.C: Terraform will check your state file to determine the provider version to use.
answers.D: Terraform will use the latest version of the provider available at the time you provision your new resource.

question_text:
You add a new resource to an existing Terraform configuration, but do not update the version constraint in the configuration. The existing and new resources use the same provider. The working directory contains a .terraform.lock.hcl file.
How will Terraform choose which version of the provider to use?

discussion.author: hbouachir
discussion.content:
B. Terraform will use the version recorded in your lock file. ✅

Here’s why:

The .terraform.lock.hcl file locks provider versions for your working directory. This ensures consistent behavior across runs, so Terraform uses the same provider version for all resources, even when you add new resources.

The version constraint in your configuration (required_providers) defines which versions are allowed, but Terraform will prioritize the version in the lock file if it satisfies the constraint.

Terraform does not check the state file to pick a provider version—the state only tracks infrastructure resources.

It does not automatically use the latest provider version unless you explicitly run terraform init -upgrade and the new version satisfies the constraints.

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 8 discussion

answers.A: Run terraform login to reauthenticate with the provider.
answers.C: Review syslog for Terraform error messages.

question_text:
terraform apply is failing with the following error. What next step should you take to determine the root cause of the problem?
Error loading state: AccessDenied: Access Denied status code: 403, request id: 288766CE5CCA24A0, host id: web.example.com

discussion.author: purink_101
discussion.content:
Setting TF_LOG=DEBUG (or TRACE) is the standard way to troubleshoot "Access Denied" errors because it reveals:
The exact URL/Bucket path Terraform is trying to reach.
The IAM identity being used for the request.
The Headers being sent, which helps you verify if a specific role or session token is being applied correctly.
If you are running this in a single command and don't want to export the variable permanently to your shell, you can run it like this:
TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform apply

discussion.author: Ranjanaj
discussion.content:
Run Terraform with debug logs to get more detailed output
TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform apply

discussion.author: LinuxChamp
discussion.content:
TF_LOG=DEBUG gives detailed provider/backend logs that will reveal the exact cause.
Answer: D

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 10 discussion

answers.A: It can execute Terraform runs on dedicated infrastructure in HCP Terraform/Terraform Cloud.
answers.B: It doesn’t show the output of a terraform apply locally.

question_text: How does the HCP Terraform/Terraform Cloud integration differ from backends such as S3, Consul, etc.?

discussion.author: purink_101
discussion.content: HCP Terraform/Terraform Cloud acts as an Enhanced Backend. Unlike "Standard" backends (S3, Consul, GCS), which only store the state file while your local CPU does the work, HCP Terraform can actually perform the execution (the plan and apply) on its own managed virtual machines. This allows for team features like Remote Execution.

discussion.author: Ajkd
discussion.content: it supports all and only available to paid customers with all features.

discussion.author: LinuxChamp
discussion.content: answer: A

answers.B: Refresh the state after running Terraform.
answers.C: Run Terraform with parallelism set to 1.
answers.D: Configure state locking for your state backend.

question_text: Which of these actions will prevent two Terraform runs from changing the same state file at the same time?

discussion.author: Ranjanaj
discussion.content:
State locking ensures that:
Only one Terraform process can modify the state at a time.
It prevents race conditions and corruption of the state file.
If a lock is in place, other runs will wait or fail until the lock is released

discussion.author: LinuxChamp
discussion.content:
Backends like S3 with DynamoDB or Consul support state locking to avoid simultaneous changes.
Answer: D

question_text: Which of these is true about Terraform’s plugin-based architecture?

discussion.author: ShakthiGCP
discussion.content: This is a core benefit of Terraform's plugin-based architecture. Terraform providers are essentially plugins that extend Terraform's capabilities to interact with various APIs (cloud providers, SaaS services, custom internal systems). If a provider doesn't exist for a specific API you need to manage, you can absolutely write your own custom provider using Go. This is a powerful extensibility feature.

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 41 discussion
question_text: Terraform can only manage resource dependencies if you set them explicitly with the depends_on argument.

discussion.author: 22801e2
discussion.content:
In Terraform, the
depends_on argument is a meta-argument used to explicitly define dependencies between resources or modules when they cannot be inferred automatically. Terraform typically manages most dependencies implicitly by analyzing attribute references in your configuration, but explicit dependency is needed in certain edge cases.

question_text: Which of the following is true about terraform apply? (Choose two.)

discussion.author: brugmany
discussion.content:
Correct Answers: B and C

A. False, you can target specific resources with -target.
B. True, changing immutable fields in AWS, Azure, etc., can cause destroy + recreate.
C. True, Terraform applies only what’s in your current working directory/workspace.
D. False, terraform apply does refresh state by default (unless you pass -refresh=false).
E. False, you can run terraform apply directly without a prior plan file. Passing a saved plan (terraform apply plan.out) is optional.

discussion.author: f0f1e05
discussion.content: B and C are correct.

discussion.author: RushinthJohn
discussion.content: Option B is false

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 56 discussion

answers.D: Using one workspace’s state as a data source for another.

question_text: Which of the following is available only in HCP Terraform workspaces and not in Terraform CLI?

discussion.author: enasuz
discussion.content: D is correct

discussion.author: luisgu
discussion.content: it's A

discussion.author: brugmany
discussion.content:
D. Using one workspace's state as a data source for another

Why this is HCP-only:
HCP Terraform supports remote state sharing between workspaces, allowing you to reference outputs from one workspace in another using the terraform_remote_state data source.

This enables cross-environment dependencies, which isn’t natively possible in standalone CLI workflows without manual state juggling.

With answer A. Secure variable storage. This is available in both CLI and HCP.

discussion.author: f0f1e05
discussion.content: A is correct answer.

question_text: Which two steps are required to provision new infrastructure in the Terraform workflow? (Choose two.)

discussion.author: ven
discussion.content: B and C. plan is helpful command but not strictly required.

discussion.author: March2023
discussion.content: B and C. If you skip plan and do an auto approve with apply, Plan still runs in the background beforehand.

discussion.author:
f0f1e05
			
				f0f1e05
			
				brugmany
discussion.content:
In my opinion, it will be B and D.B and C, sorry for previous mistakeWhy, B and C (init + apply) are correct, Plan is wrong, because plan alone doesn’t create resources.

Terraform Workflow Steps

Terraform init: sets up backend & providers. Mandatory before anything else.
Terraform plan: creates and shows you an execution plan. It’s read-only no infrastructure is changed yet. Think of it as a “preview” / dry-run.
Terraform apply: takes the plan and executes it, actually creating or modifying resources.

This is the step where provisioning happens.

discussion.author: f0f1e05
discussion.content: B and C, sorry for previous mistake

discussion.author: brugmany
discussion.content:
Why, B and C (init + apply) are correct, Plan is wrong, because plan alone doesn’t create resources.

Terraform Workflow Steps

Terraform init: sets up backend & providers. Mandatory before anything else.
Terraform plan: creates and shows you an execution plan. It’s read-only no infrastructure is changed yet. Think of it as a “preview” / dry-run.
Terraform apply: takes the plan and executes it, actually creating or modifying resources.

This is the step where provisioning happens.

discussion.content:

If you skip init, validate will fail because it won’t know where to find the providers or modules referenced in your config.

title: Exam Terraform Associate 003 topic 1 question 96 discussion

answers.A: Provisioning infrastructure in multiple cloud providers.
answers.B: Managing actions to take based on resource differences.
answers.C: Understanding API interactions with a hosted service.
answers.D: Managing resources and data sources based on an API.

question_text: What is a Terraform provider not responsible for?

discussion.author: ShakthiGCP
discussion.content:
Managing actions to take based on resource differences (The Core Terraform Engine's job): The main Terraform CLI and core engine are responsible for:
State Management: Reading the current state file.
Planning: Comparing the desired state (configuration files) with the current state (state file and real infrastructure).
Decision Making: Determining the sequence of actions needed (create, update, delete, replace) to reach the desired state.

discussion.author: March2023
discussion.content:
A single Terraform provider is dedicated to one platform.
It is not responsible for provisioning across multiple cloud providers
